# Discussion and conclusion {#sec-discussion}

```{r}
#| label: sentinel-missingness-stats
#| include: false
load_or_install <- function(pkg) {
  if (!requireNamespace(pkg, quietly = TRUE)) {
    install.packages(pkg, repos = "https://cloud.r-project.org")
  }
  suppressPackageStartupMessages(
    library(pkg, character.only = TRUE, quietly = TRUE, warn.conflicts = FALSE)
  )
}

invisible(lapply(c("dplyr", "readr", "scales"), load_or_install))

water_raw_for_missing <- readr::read_table(
  "water_temps.txt",
  col_names = c(
    "year", "month", "day", "hour", "minute",
    "wdir", "wspd", "gst", "wvht", "dpd", "apd", "mwd",
    "pres", "atmp", "wtmp", "dewp", "vis", "tide"
  ),
  skip = 2
)

sentinel_by_month <- water_raw_for_missing %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    month_num = as.integer(month),
    wtmp_C_raw = wtmp,
    is_sentinel = wtmp_C_raw %in% c(99, 999)
  ) %>%
  dplyr::group_by(month_num) %>%
  dplyr::summarise(
    n_total = dplyr::n(),
    n_sentinel = sum(is_sentinel, na.rm = TRUE),
    frac_sentinel = n_sentinel / n_total,
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    month = factor(month.abb[month_num], levels = month.abb, ordered = FALSE)
  ) %>%
  dplyr::arrange(month_num)

sentinel_overall <- water_raw_for_missing %>%
  dplyr::mutate(wtmp_C_raw = wtmp, is_sentinel = wtmp_C_raw %in% c(99, 999)) %>%
  dplyr::summarise(
    n_total = dplyr::n(),
    n_sentinel = sum(is_sentinel, na.rm = TRUE),
    frac_sentinel = n_sentinel / n_total
  )

sentinel_frac <- sentinel_overall$frac_sentinel[[1]]
sentinel_frac_jun <- sentinel_by_month$frac_sentinel[sentinel_by_month$month_num == 6][[1]]
sentinel_frac_jul <- sentinel_by_month$frac_sentinel[sentinel_by_month$month_num == 7][[1]]
sentinel_frac_aug <- sentinel_by_month$frac_sentinel[sentinel_by_month$month_num == 8][[1]]
sentinel_frac_sep <- sentinel_by_month$frac_sentinel[sentinel_by_month$month_num == 9][[1]]
```

## Interpretation in context of the business problem

Late-summer and early-fall Gulf conditions already consume enough thermal margin that restricted output is a recurring planning constraint, not a rare tail event, and within that window, top-decile load hours are over-represented among exceedances once month is accounted for (@tbl-logistic-models). For managers, the actionable read is to schedule capacity, maintenance, and commercial exposure around that predictable joint stress: markets and reliability plans should assume some high-temperature hours will coincide with the hours the system most needs full output.

## Actionable recommendations

1. **Capacity and reserves:** Incorporate expected warm-season derate exposure into resource adequacy and margin assessments, treating annualized coincident hours as a **lower bound** (see @sec-coincidence-lower-bound), not a standalone planning input.
2. **Outage and maintenance planning:** Shift discretionary work toward shoulder months and lower joint risk hours identified in the seasonal diagnostics, avoiding planned reductions when both temperature and load stress are elevated.
3. **Mitigation portfolio:** Compare cooling upgrades, thermal buffering, contractual flexibility, and demand-side options using a cost–risk screen tied to restricted-output hours and peak overlap, the quantities this report estimates directly.

## Limitations and future work

The study uses available public series and a single overlapping calendar window for joint analysis. Coincidence coverage and why annualized coincident hours are a lower bound are detailed in @sec-coincidence-lower-bound.

Buoy sensor data also contain nontrivial **sentinel missingness**: overall, `r scales::percent(sentinel_frac, accuracy = 0.1)` of half-hour `WTMP` readings are coded as sentinel values (99 or 999) and removed during cleaning, with warm-season rates of `r scales::percent(sentinel_frac_jun, accuracy = 0.1)` (Jun), `r scales::percent(sentinel_frac_jul, accuracy = 0.1)` (Jul), `r scales::percent(sentinel_frac_aug, accuracy = 0.1)` (Aug), and `r scales::percent(sentinel_frac_sep, accuracy = 0.1)` (Sep). If any material share of June–September readings are dropped, the exceedance rates reported in @tbl-monthly-water for those months should be interpreted with that caveat in mind. Moreover, sentinel missingness in buoy data is not guaranteed to be random with respect to temperature: if sensor failures correlate with extreme heat events, storm activity, or other conditions that also drive high water temperatures, then removed readings are disproportionately likely to be exceedance hours, biasing @tbl-monthly-water downward and compounding the already-noted underestimate from demand data truncation; every coincident-risk figure in this report should be understood as a conservative floor on true exposure, not a central estimate.

The 85°F threshold and top 10% peak definition are useful defaults but should be stress-tested against plant-specific heat balances and market definitions of scarcity hours.

Finally, the frequency translation implicitly assumes stationarity of the observed year. The plant was built in 1970, when an 85°F design assumption may have been reasonable; if Gulf water temperatures have trended upward since then, stationarity may be optimistic. If the business question shifts toward decadal planning, the appropriate next step is to extend the analysis using NOAA historical records (for example, longer NDBC station histories where available, or broader sea-surface temperature products such as ERSST) to quantify trends and update risk projections.

As a quantitative stress test on that assumption, NOAA NCEI reports that Gulf of Mexico sea surface temperature increased by approximately 1.0°C between **1970 and 2020** (about **0.19°C per decade**) [@noaa-ncei-gulf-warmer]. That order of magnitude implies that the distribution of warm-season temperatures driving exceedance is unlikely to be stationary over asset lifetimes: a design threshold that was exceeded only occasionally in earlier decades can plausibly become a recurrent warm-season condition today.

Where the Introduction posed the niche question, how often threshold exceedance and peak load co-occur, the discussion section answers: often enough in the observed record to treat Gulf thermal stress as a standing planning constraint, with peak hours disproportionately represented among exceedances after seasonal adjustment. The appropriate organizational response is to embed that reality in scheduling, investment, and risk governance. In practical terms, plan for a predictable warm-season window in which thermal limits can bind during high-value hours, and treat the reported coincidence metrics as conservative floors on that exposure.
